data.content:
Speech To Text Ru
Speech To Text Ru is the service used to transcribe the meeting audio into text, as indicated by the footer of the document.

Speaker 1
Speaker 1 explains the differences, structure, and requirements of analytical notes and survey reports.<SEP>Speaker 1 discusses the limitations of human error in reports compared to AI, emphasizing the need for the bot to handle decision contradictions.<SEP>Speaker 1 is a participant in the discussion who proposes ideas for document structure and chat-bot functionality.<SEP>Speaker 1 describes the functional requirements of the chatbot, emphasizing context accumulation and document retrieval capabilities.<SEP>Speaker 1 is a participant in the discussion who acts as both a functional customer and an IT specialist, explaining project requirements and roles.<SEP>Speaker 1 is a representative or expert who discusses video recording availability, transcription requirements, and the generation of analytical reports with the team.<SEP>Speaker 1 expresses satisfaction with the meeting's productivity and bids farewell to the group.

Speaker 2
Speaker 2 proposes using a functional template and explains how the system should handle conditional document sections based on project requirements.<SEP>Speaker 2 discusses interface design preferences, specifically suggesting a single canvas with editing capabilities similar to Canva.<SEP>Speaker 2 inquires about the scope of context accumulation across projects and clarifies the distinction between model selection and legislative constraints.<SEP>Speaker 2 is a participant asking questions about scaling, document details, and meeting recordings.<SEP>Speaker 2 acts as a facilitator, summarizing meeting modes and clarifying mandatory fields for document generation like protocols and analytical notes.<SEP>Speaker 2 exchanges farewells with Speaker 1 at the conclusion of the meeting.

Speaker 4
Speaker 4 clarifies that a functional template defines the structure of output documents without fixed content, allowing for dynamic generation.<SEP>Speaker 4 emphasizes the importance of UX and configuring output formats for quick user perception by analysts.<SEP>Speaker 4 questions the relevance of national legislation (FZ) to the chatbot's underlying language models, suggesting internal tools may have fewer restrictions.<SEP>Speaker 4 intervenes to suggest taking a pause and allowing others to speak.<SEP>Speaker 4 proposes concluding the meeting, organizing a video group for transcription practice, and scheduling a follow-up session by the end of the week.<SEP>Speaker 4 makes a closing remark about adding punctuation and thanks the group before continuing work in the group.

Meeting Transcript
The Meeting Transcript refers to the text generated from the meeting recording, which Speaker 1 promises to provide as a reference example for the team's testing and practice.<SEP>The Meeting Transcript is a record of a productive meeting where participants discussed protocols, added punctuation, and exchanged farewells.

Baikal Code Hackathon
The Baikal Code Hackathon is the event associated with the interview recording file mentioned in the text.

Roman
Roman is a participant addressed as "Colleague" by Speaker 4 at the beginning of the meeting.<SEP>Roman is a person who contributed an important detail regarding the distinction between the Analytical Note and the Survey Report, which caused the primary speaker to briefly digress.<SEP>Roman is a participant who contributed an important detail to the discussion regarding document types.<SEP>Roman is a participant mentioned alongside Nikita in the context of pausing for questions.<SEP>Roman correctly identified that the current session serves as a test for the participants in their role as analysts with a client.

Katya
Katya is confirmed to have connected to the team and is present on the call.

Nikita Kasyanov
Nikita Kasyanov is a representative of the Forus Group Companies participating in the meeting to discuss project tasks.

Forus Group Companies
The Forus Group Companies is the organization represented by Nikita Kasyanov, for whom the project teams are working.

Nikita Gritsanyuk
Nikita Gritsanyuk is identified as the captain of the Dream Team.

Dream Team
The Dream Team is one of three teams assigned to work on the task, led by Captain Nikita Gritsanyuk.

KFK Team
The KFK Team is one of the three project teams represented at the meeting by Ekaterina.

Ekaterina
Ekaterina represents the KFK team during this session, confirming her presence on behalf of the group.<SEP>Ekaterina is a participant waiting to ask a question about video recording availability.<SEP>Ekaterina is a participant who has been waiting to ask a question during the meeting.

Optima Team
The Optima Team is one of the three project teams assigned to the task, with Captain Yakov Vaygus.<SEP>The Optima Team is a group of participants whose inputs and questions are sought by Speaker 1 regarding their perspective on the project.

Yakov Vaygus
Yakov Vaygus is identified as the captain of the Optima team.

Junior System Analyst
The Junior System Analyst is a role responsible for preparing meeting protocols, listening to recordings, and documenting details.<SEP>The Junior System Analyst prepares the initial protocol draft but may miss details, misrepresent answers, or fail to recall specific responsibilities agreed upon with the customer.<SEP>The Junior System Analyst reviews the AI-generated draft protocol, resolves inaccuracies, and adds content before passing it to the Senior System Analyst.

Senior System Analyst
The Senior System Analyst reviews the work of the junior analyst to identify missed details or incorrect information in the protocol.<SEP>The Senior System Analyst reviews the meeting recording to identify details missed by the junior analyst, corrects formulations, and ensures accurate reflection of responsibilities and agreements.<SEP>The Senior System Analyst verifies terminology, performs a full document proofread, corrects issues, and ensures the document meets analytical standards.

Meeting Protocol Preparation Process
This method involves listening to video conference recordings, transcribing discussions, extracting open questions and decisions, and documenting them in a formal protocol document.

Video Conference Recordings
Video conference recordings are the primary source material used by analysts to review meetings and extract details for protocols.

MTR Block
The MTR block is a specific topic discussed in a meeting example, involving various tabs with differing content.

Tigran
Tigran is a person who was asked if he plans to join the meeting connection but his status remains uncertain or negative based on the context of waiting for others.

Форус
Форус is the organization where the solution will be deployed internally and which defines specific terminology for its sphere.

Никита
Никита demonstrated conceptual dictionaries and terminologies relevant to the project during a previous presentation.

Спикер 1
Спикер 1 discusses the internal deployment of the solution, access rights management, and the workflow for meeting prototyping.

Спикер 3
Спикер 3 is a learner at IVE who inquires about local access restrictions, documentation requirements, and logging mechanisms.

Спикер 7
Спикер 7 asks questions regarding the transcription of online video meetings and the inclusion of time-codes in protocols.

Екатерина
Екатерина is a participant who raises a question about meeting transcripts and their integration into the system.

LM-ка
LM-ка refers to an AI model or language machine that must adhere strictly to the client's conceptual framework and terminology when generating results.

1С Sphere
The 1С sphere is a specific industry domain characterized by a large volume of unique concepts and terms requiring precise handling.

Conceptual Dictionary
A conceptual dictionary containing specific concepts and terms for the project, which serves as a critical component in prompt design to prevent ambiguity.

Access Rights Management
Access rights management involves restricting access to the solution based on projects, potentially delegating permissions through an external information system.

IT Support Assistant
An internal assistant developed by Форус that helps employees submit requests for IT services such as software installation or hardware issues.

Chat GPT
Employees use Chat GPT for general tasks unrelated to project activities, specifically where personal data is not involved.

Laboratory
The Laboratory at Форус periodically implements new tools and assistants like the IT support agent.<SEP>The Laboratory is a resource within the organization capable of integrating the chatbot with internal APIs and systems.

Video Meeting Recording
A recording of an online meeting that serves as a primary source for creating protocols, with transcription being a potential secondary step.

Protocol
Protocols are raw records of meeting discussions that serve as the primary source for extracting information to create analytical notes and survey reports.<SEP>A protocol is a document prepared by the junior analyst following a meeting, which may include time-codes for verification before being processed by an AI agent.

AI Agent
An AI agent that processes verified protocols containing time-codes to generate analytical notes.

Junior Analyst
The Junior Analyst is responsible for preparing the meeting protocol immediately after a survey or meeting concludes.<SEP>The Junior Analyst is a potential end-user of the system, capable of modifying documents and identifying open questions.

IVE
IVE is an educational institution where Спикер 3 is currently studying.

ZOOM
Zoom is a video conferencing platform mentioned as having built-in agents capable of recording and transcribing meetings automatically.

Telemost
Telemost is the primary communication tool used by the organization for online meetings, where external cloud-based transcription services are unavailable due to security constraints.

Chatbot
The Chatbot is designed to act as a navigator for documents and meetings, retrieving information about past discussions and details without editing the source material.

GigaChat
GigaChat is a specific language model mentioned as an option compliant with Russian federal laws if such compliance is required.

Yandex Models
Models from Yandex are identified as alternatives that may be considered depending on the necessity to adhere to specific regulations.

Open II
Open source models (referred to as Open II) are suggested as viable options if legislative constraints are not a priority for the project.

Federal Laws (FZ)
Federal laws represent regulatory frameworks that might limit the choice of language models or require specific data handling protocols within the system.

NDA Agreement
The Non-Disclosure Agreement serves as a binding constraint preventing data transfer outside the company unless encrypted.

Project Documentation
Project Documentation refers to materials received from the customer, including a 36-page dictionary regulation and an analytical note, used during the survey stage.<SEP>Project documentation is uploaded to the chatbot to establish context, with versions being fixed and used for querying rather than editing.

Context Accumulation
Context accumulation refers to the process of storing and retrieving information from current or related projects to improve the chatbot's responses.

APIs
APIs are used in the initial phase to interact with various external models, allowing for flexibility before potential integration with internal systems.

Information Technology Department
The Information Technology Department is responsible for potentially connecting the chatbot's API to internal company systems upon successful development.

Accuracy Requirement
The accuracy requirement specifies that the model must correctly reflect meeting discussions, including capturing missed details or correcting misinterpretations, targeting 97%+ precision.

Speaker 6
Speaker 6 provides a brief acknowledgement ("Ugu") during the discussion but does not contribute further substantive content.<SEP>Speaker 6 acknowledges the proposal to continue working in the group and potentially schedule a follow-up meeting.

Nikita
Nikita is a speaker who interrupts to clarify whether the Analytical Note and Survey Report are identical documents or distinct ones.<SEP>Nikita is a participant in the discussion who asks for permission to interrupt and clarifies the distinction between documents.<SEP>Nikita is a project participant who provided insights on the importance of clear terminology and emphasized that developers rarely participate in the survey phase except for expert opinions.<SEP>Nikita is a participant whose name is called out by Speaker 1 during the discussion.<SEP>Nikita is an individual whose approval or availability is required to schedule another meeting before the end of the week.

Functional Customer
The Functional Customer is defined as the role responsible for defining business requirements, such as processes for coal or oil processing, distinct from IT specialists.

IT Department
The IT Department refers to a company's internal service responsible for technical support, software installation, and infrastructure maintenance.

1C RP
1C RP is described as a large-scale enterprise product implemented by companies that may exceed the capabilities of their internal IT staff.

Center For Automation And Support
The Center For Automation And Support is an organization with approximately 200 employees, including 50-60 analysts who handle high loads.

Document Review Process
The Document Review Process involves analyzing a document's structure and content to identify misinterpretations or incorrect terms, calculated by the ratio of correct pages to total pages.

Scalability
Scalability refers to the system's ability to handle increased load, potentially through monolithic expansion or horizontal scaling with containerization support.

Video Recording
A video recording of the meeting is a potential resource requested by participants for verifying transcription accuracy during development.<SEP>The Video Recording refers to the captured footage of the meeting, sought by developers for testing transcription accuracy and system validation.

System Analyst
The System Analyst (Junior or Senior) is the specialist responsible for creating PPM schemes and refining details in analytical notes.<SEP>A junior or senior system analyst who works with the tool and may also serve as a functional customer.

Accountant
An accountant identified as an example of a user who would work within the 1C RP product rather than the IT department.

Director
A director mentioned as another potential user who might access the 1C RP system for management purposes.

Bakery Owner
An example of a business owner (e.g., of a bakery) who understands product details like pirog pricing but lacks IT knowledge.

Pirog
A type of food item used as an example for inventory tracking and cost calculation in non-IT projects.

Customer
The Customer is a stakeholder who agrees on responsibilities and answers; their feedback is crucial for correcting misunderstandings and ensuring protocols match actual discussions.

Project Team Members
Members of the project team may inadvertently record assumptions made before or after meetings as facts within the protocol, leading to inaccuracies if not verified with the customer.

Meeting Protocol
The Meeting Protocol is a document intended to accurately capture discussion details, responsibilities, and agreements; it often suffers from delays in preparation due to analyst workload and context switching.<SEP>A document recording meeting details, including mandatory fields like date, agenda, participants, and optional sections like open questions or stamps.

Analytical Note
The Analytical Note is a summary document combining information from all meeting protocols, typically ranging up to 50 pages, prepared at the end of the survey phase.<SEP>An analytical note is typically a structured document with a volume of up to 50 pages, containing processed information from protocols including functional requirements and process models.<SEP>The Analytical Note is a document prepared by analysts based on project documentation to define requirements and scope.<SEP>A document prepared after all meeting protocols are finalized and approved by the client, based on the collected documentation.<SEP>A generated document that can be refined by analysts and used to retrain the underlying model based on new data.<SEP>The Analytical Note is a final document that requires at least one protocol as a mandatory field for generation.

Survey Report
The Survey Report is a comprehensive document exceeding 50 pages in length, distinct from the Analytical Note but serving a similar purpose of summarizing the survey results.<SEP>A survey report is a larger document ranging from 50 to over 200 pages, often detailing extensive findings and current state descriptions.

1C Object Terminology
1C Object Terminology refers to specific professional terms used by analysts (e.g., 'nomenclature catalog') instead of simple language when documenting product details like goods and services.

Meeting Recording Assistant
The Meeting Recording Assistant is a proposed AI tool designed to process meeting recordings or transcripts to generate draft protocols, saving time for analysts by highlighting unclear sections.

Forest
Forest is an internal company where the speakers work and which has conducted internal projects involving automation and analytical note creation.

47-Page Document
A specific example of an internal analytical note mentioned by the speaker, consisting of 47 pages, which is currently being edited before it can be shared.

Functional Requirements Registry
A registry listing functional requirements, categorized by classes such as data migration or integrations, with links to specific protocols where they were discussed.

Business Process Description
A section in the analytical note describing current business processes, often presented in table format or indicated for PPM scheme creation.

MTR File
An external document used as a source of information regarding current data views and naming conventions within the project context.

Project Charter
A foundational project document that may contain additional information included in the analytical note.<SEP>The Project Charter is a document containing terminology and details (e.g., regulations, clauses) used as context for protocol generation.

PPM Scheme
A visual representation of processes that is complex enough to require manual preparation by a system analyst rather than an automated assistant.

Assistant
An automated tool discussed as a potential aid to highlight where analysts should work, but not capable of drawing complex schemes itself.

1C ERP
1C ERP is a target system mentioned where data from the new system is sent for catalog updates.

Russian Classifier
The Russian Classifier is identified as a specific group of catalogs within the project context.

Target Model
The Target Model represents how the customer views the data and system state after implementation.

Legacy System
The Legacy System is an existing system that will remain in operation even after new implementation, receiving some data transfers.

Attribute Composition
Attribute composition is a specific section of the analytical note detailing data fields and structures, noted as project-specific.

AI Assistant
The AI Assistant is a tool intended to handle monotonous routine tasks, generate protocols, and suggest process descriptions or schemas.<SEP>The AI Assistant is a tool that processes meeting recordings to generate draft protocols, providing timestamps and confidence levels for uncertain sections.

Project Team Leader
The Project Team Leader oversees the protocol's compliance with client formatting requirements and general structure, focusing on client communication rather than technical architecture.

Meeting Recording
A recording of a meeting that serves as the primary source material for generating draft protocols due to its reliability compared to transcriptions.

Draft Protocol
An initial output generated by the AI Assistant containing potential inaccuracies, timestamps for review areas, and confidence indicators.

Finalized Protocol
The complete, reviewed, and agreed-upon document resulting from the collaborative work of the project team members.

1C Project Management System
An internal project management system used to store final versions of analytical notes, technical specifications, and protocols.

Bitrix
A communication platform previously used for Skype and currently utilized for private messages and chat interactions within the project team.

Telegram
A messaging application used by the teams for direct communication, group chats, and coordination between client and contractor sides.

Skype
A communication platform that was used by the team until recently but has since been disconnected.

Speaker 5
Speaker 5 is a participant who briefly checks audio connectivity during the meeting.<SEP>Speaker 5 provides brief acknowledgments during the discussion about mandatory protocols.

Senior Analyst
The Senior Analyst is a potential end-user who needs to quickly perceive contradictions and nuances in the document output.

Client
The Client participates in the external group and provides documentation such as project charters, regulations, and product specifications.<SEP>The Client is a stakeholder that analysts can contact via chat if they encounter open questions or contradictions in the documents.

Document System
A proposed system for creating, analyzing, and storing meeting protocols with features to detect contradictions and support user interaction.

Chatbot Interface
An interface design concept where users interact via a chat window (like ChatGPT) while viewing documents on the side or below.

Single Canvas Interface
An alternative interface design featuring one unified document area for editing and a separate chat area for instructions.

Contradictions
Conflicting information within documents that the system must detect, highlight, or report as open questions.

Open Questions
Unresolved issues identified in documents that require clarification from the client or further discussion.

UX (User Experience)
A design principle focused on making document outputs readable and meaningful for users like analysts.

DeepS
An example of a chatbot interface mentioned by Speaker 1 as a potential model for the system's design.

LM Notebook
An existing application referenced by Speaker 1 that features a layout with documentation on the left, a chat in the center, and output options on the right.

Canva
A platform mentioned as an example of a tool where users can edit documents directly within a single canvas view.

Date Field
A mandatory field within the meeting protocol structure used to record the date of the meeting.

Agenda
A mandatory component of the meeting protocol that outlines the topics for discussion.

Participants
Mandatory information in the protocol listing the individuals who attended the meeting.

Definitions Section
An optional section containing terms, definitions, abbreviations, and notations that may be included if necessary.

Meeting Content Section
A mandatory section of the protocol detailing the substance of the meeting discussions.

Questions And Solutions Section
A section often combining questions and solutions, though it can be split or omitted depending on clarity.

Stamp Of Agreement
A standard element in protocols indicating approval, typically marked with a stamp.

Speaker 3
Speaker 3 asks questions regarding the public placement of links for testing purposes.

Speaker 7
Speaker 7 asks if a video recording of the meeting exists for use in development and verification processes.

Project Protocol
The Project Protocol is a document generated from meeting discussions, which must be agreed upon by the user before generating an analytical note.

Transcription
Transcription is the process of converting video recordings into text, considered an essential requirement for generating protocols in this system.

Telegram Group
A Telegram group is used by the executor's team (e.g., Dream Team or Optima) for internal communication separate from the main project group.<SEP>The Telegram Group is a digital communication channel created by Speaker 4 where meeting videos and subsequent questions will be shared.

Internal Project
An Internal Project refers to confidential company projects from which usable video recordings for testing can be sourced, unlike external client projects.

Report On Inspection
A Report on Inspection is a document type similar to an analytical note that requires protocols as mandatory fields for generation.

Old System
The Old System is a legacy system that continues to receive some data during the transition phase.

New System (1C ERP)
The New System, specifically 1C ERP, is the target platform where updated reference book data is sent from the current operations.

Survey Stage
The Survey Stage involves thoroughly investigating the customer's problems, needs, and situation (Ziz) and accounts for 30-40% of total project time.

Modeling Stage
The Modeling Stage encompasses preparation of technical specifications and system design, which follows the survey stage.

Development Stage
The Development Stage involves programmers creating system objects based on previous analysis; errors here can lead to significant financial risks if not properly surveyed.

Technical Specification (TZ)
The Technical Specification is a detailed document (sometimes up to 120 pages) containing requirements and object definitions, formed after modeling and surveying.

BPMN Schema
A BPMN schema is a visual representation of business processes that may be recommended by an AI assistant for analysts to create.

Forum (Customer)
The Forum represents the customer whose specific terminology and conceptual framework must be strictly adhered to in project deliverables.

1C Domain
The 1C Domain refers to a specific industry sector with a large volume of unique concepts and terminology that AI systems must understand.

Legal Dispute
A Legal Dispute is a potential negative outcome where the executor and customer argue in court over unclear requirements stemming from poor surveying.

Project Group
The Project Group is an internal team consisting only of the executor's representatives, used for specific project discussions.

External Team
The External Team includes both the client and the executors, serving as a broader group for project collaboration.

Executor
The Executor is responsible for implementing the project and uses internal groups to manage their specific tasks.

Transcripts
Transcripts are recordings of interviews with the client that are uploaded into a system for processing.

Interviews
Interviews with the client generate data (transcripts and context) used to create meeting protocols.

Meeting Protocols
Meeting Protocols are documents generated from interviews that summarize discussions, decisions, and questions.

Regulations
Regulations are reference documents consulted by analysts to find specific answers during meetings or documentation work.

Bot Assistant
The Bot Assistant is an AI tool designed to learn from context, generate interview protocols, and identify potential contradictions in project decisions.

Functional Template
A Functional Template defines the structure (sections) for output documents without fixed content, allowing for dynamic generation based on project requirements.

PDF Document
The PDF document contains the general assignment and goals for the project, including the objective to eliminate human errors in reporting.

Forum Project
The Forum Project is a specific instance where the client requirements dictate that some report sections may be optional or conditional.

1C System
The 1C system is a source for data exports and product specifications used to provide context to the bot assistant.

Transcribed Meeting
The content of the meeting transcript was generated using the Speech To Text Ru service.

Client Users
The availability of end users (clients) directly influences the scheduling gaps and delays between meetings in the project plan.

Meeting Schedule
The availability of end users (clients) directly influences the scheduling gaps and delays between meetings in the project plan.

Logging Mechanism
Спикер 3 questions whether logging mechanisms need to be configured to ensure proper product usage tracking.

Missed Details
The system is designed to identify and quantify missed details in meeting discussions as part of its accuracy validation process.

Encrypted Data
Speaker 1 specifies that external data transfer is permitted only if the data is encrypted to satisfy security constraints.

Incorrect Terms
The system checks for misinterpreted concepts or incorrectly selected terms within the document context.

Sanitized Documentation
Speaker 1 provided anonymized documentation examples to ensure no personal or organizational data leaks during the task demonstration.

Internal APIs
The Chatbot is designed to replace current API connections with internal company APIs upon final system implementation.

Red Pages
The system evaluates performance by counting "red pages" (pages with significant errors) as a metric of incorrect interpretations.

Business Process
Functional customers provide critical details about business processes (e.g., coal burning efficiency) that IT staff may not know.

Containerization
Horizontal scalability is discussed as a potential technical solution involving containerization support.

Accuracy Calculation
The accuracy of the document review is calculated by comparing incorrect pages to total pages (e.g., 1 red page out of 4 equals 75% accuracy).

Internal Projects
The company Forest conducts internal projects focused on automation where analytical notes are generated as part of the process.

New System
Data flows from the new system into 1C ERP specifically for updating catalog information.

Project Context
The Russian Classifier constitutes a distinct category of catalogs that were highlighted in the project requirements.

Client Requirements
The Project Team Leader ensures the protocol aligns with client requirements and maintains the correct document structure based on client communication.

Architecture Details
The Project Team Leader is noted to lack deep understanding of technical architecture details, focusing instead on client requirements and general structure.

Model
Refined analytical notes can be fed back into the model to train it on new data and improve performance.

Model Training
The analytical note serves as training data that can be fed back into the model to improve its future performance and accuracy.

Meeting Session
Roman identifies that the current meeting session functions as a practical test where participants act as analysts interacting with a client representative.

Technical Specification
The Modeling Stage utilizes survey data to prepare the Technical Specification (TZ) for developers.

Research Phase
The Survey Stage is also referred to as the Research Phase; they are synonyms used interchangeably by project members to describe the investigation of customer needs and problems (Ziz).

Preparation Of TZ
The Modeling Stage is divided into two distinct sub-sectors: the Preparation of Technical Specifications (TZ) and the Design phase.

Design Phase
The Modeling Stage includes a specific section called the Design Phase, which focuses on creating the future system's architecture for developers.

Analyst
Analysts consult Regulations to find answers and resolve questions during meetings or documentation work.

Product Specifications
The Client provides product specifications (e.g., from 1C) as additional context for the project.

Output Document
Speaker 4 defines that the output document requires a fixed structural template (functional sections) but flexible internal content.
